revision.text:
'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{nihongo|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 19qq (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{Coord|33|40|32.9|N|135|58|15.6|E|region:JP|display=title}}

== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{nihongo|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 19qq (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{nihongo|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 19qq (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.

revision.text:
'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{nihongo|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 19qq (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{nihongo|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 19qq (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{nihongo|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 19qq (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{nihongo|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 19qq (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})

revision.text:
'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{nihongo|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 19qq (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 19qq (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})

A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})

revision.text:
'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

[[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Sano Ōji (Shingu)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Sano Ōji (Shingu)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Sano Ōji (Shingu)]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Sano Ōji (Shingu)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sano Ōji (Shingu)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sano Ōji (Shingu)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sano Ōji (Shingu)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:ano Ōji (Shingu)]][[Category:11]] ]] [[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]][[zh:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[sn:ano Ōji (Shingu)]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Sano Ōji (Shingu)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Sano Ōji (Shingu)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sano Ōji (Shingu)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sano Ōji (Shingu)]][[Category:qq]]

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})

== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}

It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})

revision.text:
'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>
