revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery>== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})

revision.text:
'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery> == See Also! == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery> == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.

revision.text:
'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery> == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Kujūku Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}

'''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Kujūku Ōji|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|ja|紀伊佐野駅}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery> == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji}})

revision.text:
[[Image:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|thumb|200px|佐野王子址碑]]
'''佐野王子'''（さのおうじ）は[[和歌山県]][[新宮市]]にある[[神社]]旧址。熊野[[九十九王子]]のひとつ。県指定史跡（[[1959年]]〈[[昭和]]34年〉1月8日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |publisher=和歌山県教育委員会 |accessdate=2009-03-21 }}</ref>。
== 概要 == 佐野王子の創建年代は明らかではなく、[[承元]]4年（[[1210年]]）の[[藤原重子|修明門院]]参詣記に初出する<ref name="Oohechi">紀南文化財研究会・熊野歴史研究会[2008: 88]</ref>。[[江戸時代|江戸期]]の地誌『[[紀伊続風土記]]』は、[[熊野那智大社]]の境外[[末社]]となった後、廃絶したと伝えている。それによれば、佐野王子の跡地は若一王子森と呼ばれており、周囲230[[間]]（約420メートル）の立派な森であったという<ref name="Oohechi"/>。
しかし、他の記録には、本来の佐野王子とは近くの王子川河畔に設けられた祓所が転じたものであるとされ、この祓所を継承したと見られる若一王子神社が[[明治]]まで存続していた。この神社は、[[1911年]]（明治44年）[[10月26日]]付で、新宮市佐野山田の天御中主神社（あめのみなかぬし）神社に合祀廃絶された<ref name="Nishi">西[1987: 168]</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">長谷川[2007: 160-161]</ref>。その後も[[1926年]]（大正15年）の和歌山県の調査では、王子橋から約2[[町 (単位)|町]]（約230m）の松林の中に3[[尺]]（約90cm）四方の小祠があったとしているが、『新宮市誌』<ref>[新宮市 1937]</ref>には倒壊して失われたと述べられている<ref name="Nishi"/>。
現在地は王子橋の北約80メートルの位置にあり、「王子跡」と刻銘された石碑および説明版、さらに碑の北側約10メートルに[[北条政子]]の宝筐印塔、地蔵群、南側約40メートルに神武天皇聖績狭野顕彰碑がある<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>。
== 所在地 == * 和歌山県新宮市佐野641 : 周囲は[[スーパーセンターオークワ南紀店]]になっており、王子森の面影は見られない。
== 交通機関 == * [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[紀勢本線]]・[[紀伊佐野駅]]下車 : 国道42号線に出て、那智方面へ約300メートル * [[熊野交通]]バスで「王子橋」バス停下車、国道42号線山側
== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book ja-jp|editor=紀南文化財研究会・熊野歴史研究会|year=2008|title=熊野古道大辺路調査報告書 - 田辺市から新宮市まで|publisher=大辺路再生実行委員会}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=新宮市|year=1937|title=新宮市誌|publisher=新宮市}} → 1984、新興出版社 * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=西 律|year=1987|title=熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録|publisher=荒尾成文堂|series=みなもと選書1}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=長谷川 靖高|year=2007|title=熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック|publisher=新風書房|isbn=9784882696292}}
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kujuku-ojis|九十九王子}} * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[中辺路]] * [[九十九王子]] : （[[浜王子]]） - '''佐野王子''' - （[[熊野三所大神社|浜の宮王子]]）
{{九十九王子}} {{神道 横}} {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|40|32.9|N|135|58|15.6|E|region:JP|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さのおうし}} [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:新宮市の歴史]] [[Category:新宮市の建築物]] [[Category:九十九王子]] [[Category:和歌山県指定史跡]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Shingu|qid=Q823526}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11385071]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * [[:en:Kii-Sano Station|Kii-Sano Station]] (JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery> == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|qid=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|qq=|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hama Ōji|qq=|1=Hama Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11557908}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|qq=}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Shingu|WD=Q823526}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11385071]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * [[:en:Kii-Sano Station|Kii-Sano Station]] (JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Shingu|WD=Q823526}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11385071]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * [[:en:Kii-Sano Station|Kii-Sano Station]] (JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Shingu|WD=Q823526}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11385071]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * [[:en:Kii-Sano Station|Kii-Sano Station]] (JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Shingu|WD=Q823526}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11385071]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * [[:en:Kii-Sano Station|Kii-Sano Station]] (JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Shingu|WD=Q823526}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11385071]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * [[:en:Kii-Sano Station|Kii-Sano Station]] (JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery> == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hama Ōji|1=Hama Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11557908}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|qq=}})

== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * [[:en:Kii-Sano Station|Kii-Sano Station]] (JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery> == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hama Ōji|1=Hama Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11557908}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|qq=}})

== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}

== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * [[:en:Kii-Sano Station|Kii-Sano Station]] (JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.

== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * [[:en:Kii-Sano Station|Kii-Sano Station]] (JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
