<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Shingu|WD=Q823526}}}} '''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|en|Kii-Sano Station|WD=Q2584973|lt=Kii-Sano Station}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery> == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|1=Hama Ōji Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hama Ōji|1=Hama Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11557908}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|qq=}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}

== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=list of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|en|Kii-Sano Station|WD=Q2584973|lt=Kii-Sano Station|1=Kii-Sano Station}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery> == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|1=Hama Ōji Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hama Ōji|1=Hama Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11557908}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|qq=}})

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Shingu|WD=Q823526|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}}} '''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=list of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|en|Kii-Sano Station|WD=Q2584973|lt=Kii-Sano Station|1=Kii-Sano Station}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Shingu|WD=Q823526|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}}} '''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=list of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|en|Kii-Sano Station|WD=Q2584973|lt=Kii-Sano Station|1=Kii-Sano Station}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery> == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|1=Hama Ōji Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hama Ōji|1=Hama Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11557908}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|qq=}})

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:佐野王子 (新宮市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Shingu|WD=Q823526|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}}} '''Sano Ōji (Shingu)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Sano Ōji == '''Sano Ōji''' (佐野王子) is the former site of a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It was one of the {{nihongo|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji}}, auxiliary shrines associated with the {{nihongo|Kumano Kōdō|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage routes. The site is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=list of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} since January 8, 1959.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The exact date of Sano Ōji's founding is unknown, but it first appears in historical records in 1210 (Shōgen 4), within the ''Pilgrimage Record of Empress Shumeimon-in''.<ref name="Oohechi">Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association (2008), p. 88</ref> According to the Edo-period topography ''{{nihongo|Kii Zoku Fudoki|紀伊続風土記}}'', the shrine became a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}} before falling into disuse. The site was known as ''Wakaichi Ōji-no-Mori,'' a distinguished forest with a circumference of about 420 meters.<ref name="Oohechi"/>
Some records suggest that the original Sano Ōji was not at its current location but rather near the banks of the Ōji River, where a purification site (''haraisho'') once existed. This purification site likely evolved into {{nihongo|Wakaichi Ōji Shrine|若一王子神社|qq=draft}}, which remained active until the Meiji era. The shrine was officially merged with {{nihongo|Ame-no-Minakanushi Shrine|天御中主神社}} in Shingū’s Sano Yamada district on October 26, 1911 (Meiji 44), leading to its abolition.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 168</ref><ref name="Hasegawa">Hasegawa (2007), pp. 160–161</ref>
A 1926 (Taishō 15) Wakayama Prefectural survey recorded a small shrine, roughly 90 cm square, in a pine forest about 230 meters from Ōji Bridge. However, by the time of the publication of the ''Shingū City Gazetteer,'' it had collapsed and disappeared.<ref name="Nishi"/>
Today, the site is located about 80 meters north of Ōji Bridge. It features a stone monument inscribed with "Ōji Site" (王子跡), an informational plaque, and nearby relics such as a pagoda-shaped stupa (''hōkyōintō'') attributed to {{nihongo|Hōjō Masako|北条政子}}, a group of Jizō statues, and a monument commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s legendary deeds.<ref name="Nishi"/><ref name="Hasegawa"/>
== Location == * Address: 641 Sano, Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan * The area is now part of a commercial complex, with little trace of the former sacred forest (Ōji-no-Mori).
== Access == * {{ill|Kii-Sano Station|en|Kii-Sano Station|WD=Q2584973|lt=Kii-Sano Station|1=Kii-Sano Station}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}) : Exit to National Route 42, then walk approximately 300 meters toward Nachi. * {{nihongo|Kumano Kōtsū|熊野交通}} Bus: Get off at "Ōji Bridge" (王子橋) stop and walk inland along Route 42.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:SANO-oji remains monument.JPG|Stone monument marking the Sano Ōji site </gallery> == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} – {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} : ({{ill|Hama Ōji|ja|浜王子|1=Hama Ōji Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hama Ōji|1=Hama Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11557908}}) – '''Sano Ōji''' – ({{ill|Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|ja|熊野三所大神社|en|lt2=浜の宮王子|lt=Hama-no-Miya Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha|qq=}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor=Kinan Cultural Property Research Association, Kumano Historical Research Association|year=2008|title=Survey Report on the Kumano Kōdō Ōhechi Route: From Tanabe to Shingū|publisher=Ōhechi Revitalization Committee}} * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City Gazetteer|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished 1984, Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|author=Nishi, Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Guide: A Field Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa, Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Publishing|isbn=9784882696292}}
